experiences.intentions:
Player 1: No role-specific hidden info as a Servant. On round 1 with no prior data, a 2-person team including myself and the proposer is standard and low-risk; approving helps generate information and avoids unnecessary early rejects. My vote reveals little beyond a reasonable good-faith default.
Player 3: As a Servant, I have no private information yet. On round 1 quest 1, a 2-person team of [1,2] is fairly standard, and rejecting without evidence gives up a low-cost information opportunity. Approving reveals quest outcome info; if it fails, both become suspicious, and if it passes, it modestly clears them without strongly harming Good.
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil, so 1 and 2 are both non-seen-as-evil and this 2-player team excludes known evil. On quest 1, approving a clean-looking team is optimal for Good and does not reveal much beyond standard pro-good behavior. Since I proposed [1,2], rejecting my own solid team would add confusion without benefit.
Player 0: I know player 4 is my fellow evil teammate; players 1 and 2 are not confirmed evil, so this proposed team excludes all known evil. As Minion on the first team vote, optimal play is often to approve a clean-looking team to avoid outing myself and to preserve flexibility, especially since a reject on an all-good team can be suspicious. Approving reveals little and helps me blend in with likely good players' behavior.
Player 4: I know Player 0 is my evil teammate; players 1, 2, and 3 are good. The proposed team [1,2] contains no evil, so if approved it will likely succeed and give good an early clean quest. As evil on team voting, I should generally reject all-good teams to try to get an evil player onto the mission later and avoid confirming good players. My reject reveals little because early round 1 votes are weak information, and opposing this team helps evil more than approving it.

Player 3 now adds themselves to make [1,2,3]; with only one successful 2-person quest so far, this is a fairly standard and reasonable expansion. As a good Servant in team voting, I should generally approve teams that preserve the successful core unless there is stronger suspicion. Approving reveals I support continuity from the known success, which helps good more than rejecting an otherwise solid team.
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil, so 1 and 3 are good. The proposed team [1,2,3] contains only good players, making it optimal to approve. Approving also looks natural because the prior successful team [1,2] is expanded with another good player; this reveals little beyond standard good play and helps secure a likely success on quest 2.
Player 3: Quest 1 with team [1,2] succeeded, so 1 and 2 are mildly good-leaning but not cleared. As a Servant, I want to approve reasonable teams that preserve successful members; adding myself to [1,2] is a strong low-risk team for a 3-person quest. Rejecting would give little information and slow good tempo. My approve vote is standard and does not meaningfully help evil more than rejecting would.
Player 4: Players 1 and 2 were on the successful first quest, which weakly suggests they could be good or that evil chose not to fail; with only me and Player 0 as evil, team [1,2,3] contains no evil and is therefore very likely to succeed. As evil in team voting, the optimal play is to reject clean all-good teams, especially early when there is little downside to opposing them. My reject gives limited information because evil often oppose good-looking teams, but approving would help good secure another success immediately.
Player 0: I know 4 is my evil teammate; 1,2,3 are all ostensibly good from my perspective, and the first successful quest with [1,2] gives no direct clears but suggests no evil was on it. Current phase is team voting on [1,2,3]. As evil, I generally want to avoid openly rejecting all-good teams in early game if that makes me stand out, especially since this is only round 1 and the team includes none of the evil players. Approving can help me blend in with the likely majority and preserve cover. My vote is private except for aggregate count, so approving helps hide alignment better than a conspicuous reject here.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 4 is Evil. Team [1,4] includes a known Evil, so as Good I should reject to prevent an evil player going on quest 3. Voting reject is also consistent with protecting hidden information; it signals the team is bad without explicitly outing my knowledge more than necessary, since including 4 after two clean quests is broadly suspicious anyway.
Player 3: Two successful quests with teams [1,2] and [1,2,3] mean players 1, 2, and likely 3 are strongly good-coded; at minimum, neither prior team contained a fail. As a Servant, optimal play is to favor continuity with proven-success players. Proposed team [1,4] drops both 2 and me and introduces untested 4, which adds unnecessary risk on quest 3. Rejecting signals preference for stronger evidence-based teams and helps good avoid giving evil an opening.
Player 1: I am Good Servant. Both prior quests succeeded with teams [1,2] and [1,2,3], so players 1,2,3 look relatively trustworthy, though double-success doesn't fully clear them. Player 4 has no positive data yet, and this proposed 2-player team [1,4] for quest 3 is unusual because on 5-player Avalon the third quest should require 2 players, so size is legal; still, swapping out previously successful members for an untested 4 adds risk. As Good, optimal play is to approve teams with stronger evidence. Rejecting preserves information and pressures leader to propose a safer team like [1,2] or [1,3]. My vote reveals I prefer evidence-based teams and does not materially hurt Good.
Player 0: I know Player 4 is evil with me. Quests 1 and 2 both succeeded with teams [1,2] and [1,2,3], so 1 and 2 look relatively good, while 3 is at least not proven evil. The proposed team [1,4] includes my evil teammate on a 2-person quest. As evil, getting an evil player onto the team is valuable, and approving avoids signaling against my teammate. Even though a fail would expose 4 somewhat, evil needs to start converting quest losses now that good is up 2-0; approving gives us that chance. My vote reveals some support for 4, but with only one evil on the team it is still the optimal aggressive play.
Player 4: I know 0 is my evil teammate; 1,2,3 are good unless one is Merlin/Percival. Quests 1 and 2 both succeeded with teams [1,2] and [1,2,3], so 1/2/3 look trusted by table. Current phase is team voting on my proposed team [1,4] for quest 3. As evil Assassin on the team, approving gives me access to fail this quest and stop the good streak. Rejecting my own team would look highly suspicious and likely lose a strong sabotage opportunity. Approving reveals consistency with proposing the team and does not hurt evil; if team passes, I can choose fail on quest.

Player 2: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,1] includes a known evil, so as Good I should reject it to avoid risking a fail on quest 3. Rejecting also gives useful signal consistent with my prior successful team choices without exposing too much beyond opposing an obviously bad team.
Player 3: Two successful quests used players 1,2 and then 1,2,3, so players 1 and 2 look especially good; I know I am good, making 1,2,3 a very strong core. Proposed team [0,1] drops two proven-good members from the last success and includes untested 0. As a good servant in team voting, I should prefer teams strongly supported by prior quest data, especially on quest 3 where a fail would swing momentum. Rejecting also signals preference for the successful core and with only round 2 there is little risk in seeking a better team.
Player 0: I am evil Minion with teammate 4. Quests 1 and 2 succeeded with teams [1,2] and [1,2,3], so 1/2/3 look relatively good to others; 4 is somewhat suspect after proposing [1,4] and getting rejected. Current phase is team voting on my proposed team [0,1]. As evil, getting an evil player onto quest 3 is valuable because with only 2 players on team, a single fail would stop the good sweep and create confusion. Since I am on the team, I should approve. Approving is consistent with being leader who proposed it and does not reveal extra information beyond normal self-interest.
Player 4: Players 1 and 2 were on the first successful 2-person quest; players 1,2,3 were on the second successful 3-person quest. That makes 1/2/3 look good to the table, while my evil teammate 0 has not been on a quest yet. As Assassin on evil, with good up 2-0, we need to start getting evil onto quests. The current team [0,1] includes one evil (0) and one broadly trusted good-looking player (1), so it is one of our best chances to get a fail through while still appearing plausible. Approving helps evil by putting 0 on mission; rejecting likely pushes toward another all-good-looking team. My approve also aligns with 0 likely approving and is not especially revealing since leaders often back their own team and evil wants mixed teams through.
Player 1: I am Good Servant. Two quests have already succeeded with teams [1,2] and [1,2,3], which makes Players 1, 2, and likely 3 look relatively good, while 0 and 4 are less cleared. On quest 3 with a 2-person team, sending [0,1] adds an uncleared player and excludes the strongest prior-success core. As Good, optimal play is to reject riskier teams and prefer teams built from previously successful members. My vote signals skepticism toward introducing 0 without evidence, which helps Good preserve reliable team composition.
